ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 covers kimchi jjigae as a category; no separate code exists specifically for pork kimchi soup variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers kimchi jjigae broadly; pork-specific variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for kimchi jjigae or pork kimchi soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of pork kimchi soup is approximately 400 g, typically served as a single-portion bowl with rice on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Kimchi jjigae (kimchi stew with pork) — National Institute of Agricultural Sciences (NAAS) Korean Food Composition Table, 9th revision; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central composite for pork-based soups
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Agricultural Sciences (NAAS), Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from the Korean NAAS Food Composition Table 9th revision entry for kimchi jjigae (돼지고기 김치찌개) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central data for pork-based stews. The NAAS entry is the primary source; USDA data used for secondary nutrient cross-check. Values represent a typical restaurant-style preparation with pork belly or shoulder, fermented kimchi, tofu, and seasonings.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Korean traditional foods estimated from mixed stew composition; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for vegetable and meat stew
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork kimchi soup (kimchi jjigae) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed meat-and-vegetable stew entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The dish is low in refined carbohydrates; the primary carbohydrate contributors are kimchi (fermented cabbage, low GI ~15) and small amounts of tofu. The composite GI is estimated conservatively at 35, placing it firmly in the low category (≤55). Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 3.2 × 4) / 100 = 4.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fermented kimchi and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a 400 g serving provides approximately 1920 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork kimchi soup has a low estimated GI of 35 and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large portions of accompanying white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from pork and elevated sodium from fermented kimchi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (1.3 g per 100 g) from pork and high sodium (~480 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean pork cuts, limit serving size, and balance with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Kimchi Soup (돼지고기 김치찌개, Dwaeji-gogi Kimchi-jjigae) is a quintessential Korean stew made by simmering well-fermented kimchi with pork (typically belly or shoulder), tofu, garlic, gochugaru (Korean red chilli flakes), and sesame oil in a savoury broth. The dish is deeply umami-rich, spicy, and tangy, with a robust red colour derived from the kimchi and chilli. The texture combines tender pork pieces, silken or firm tofu, and softened kimchi leaves in a flavourful, slightly thick broth. It is one of the most widely consumed home-cooked and restaurant dishes in Korea and is commonly served with steamed white rice and banchan (side dishes). Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, high-protein, high-sodium food. Per 100 g it provides approximately 68 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 3.8 g fat, and 3.2 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high (~480 mg per 100 g) due to the fermented kimchi and seasoning pastes, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (~35) makes it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when rice intake is moderated. Kimchi also contributes probiotics, vitamin C, and dietary fibre, adding functional food value.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kimchi stew?
Is kimchi soup good for diabetics?
How much sodium is in kimchi jjigae?
Korean pork and kimchi stew nutrition

Can I eat kimchi soup if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the Korean NAAS Food Composition Table 9th revision for kimchi jjigae (돼지고기 김치찌개), which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable mixed meat-and-vegetable stews, as no direct GI measurement for pork kimchi soup exists in the approved GI database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted nutrient match and estimated GI.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the NAAS Korean Food Composition Table for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in pork-based Korean stews per NAAS data. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. The GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement from the University of Sydney GI Database is not available for this specific dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 3.2 × 4) / 100 = 4.5 for a 400 g serving. Sodium content is notably high and is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.
